name: IO Monad

x.formalRole: Represents an effectful action as a first-class, deferred value that is only executed at the program’s edge.
x.compositionRule: IO a is a description of an effect; bind sequences descriptions; nothing runs until the runtime executes it.
laws: Monad laws hold; referential transparency preserved because IO values are descriptions, not executions
x.encoding: Lazy thunks / effect objects (Haskell IO, Scala cats-effect IO / ZIO). In eager languages, a () → a thunk or task approximates it.
x.useCases: Keeping a pure core while pushing side effects (I/O, time, randomness) to a controlled boundary.
misuseRisks: Running effects eagerly, defeating the point
Wrapping everything in IO until the type is meaningless

def: A monad that captures side effects as deferred, composable description values, executed only at the program boundary — preserving a pure core.
problem: Reconcile real-world side effects (I/O, clocks, randomness) with referential transparency and easy reasoning/testing.
mechanism: An effect becomes a first-class IO value (a description). bind builds bigger descriptions by sequencing; the runtime "interprets" the final IO at main. Because values are descriptions, equational reasoning still holds.
benefits: Pure core, effects at the edge
failures: Forcing eager execution and losing purity
whenNot: Small scripts where direct effects are fine
Hot paths where the abstraction cost matters
myths: "The IO monad makes side effects pure" — it makes *descriptions* of effects pure; execution still happens
